extracted_rules.condition: When prescribing sleep for any athlete in active training
extracted_rules.action: Target 8-9 hours of sleep per night; minimum 7 hours; below 6 hours triggers mandatory training modification
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Target: 8-9h/night; minimum: 7h; <6h: flag as critical — reduce session intensity by 20%, no high-intensity sessions
extracted_rules.source: Sleep review: circadian rhythm and sleep in young adult athletes (original source)

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete has accumulated sleep debt during a training week
extracted_rules.action: Prescribe 1 hour of additional sleep for each 2 hours of weekly sleep deficit; do not attempt to recover >3h of sleep debt in a single night
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Compensation rate: 1h extra sleep per 2h deficit; weekly deficit cap for single-night recovery: ≤3h; multi-night recovery preferred
extracted_rules.source: Sleep debt research consensus; circadian rhythm and performance literature

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete has a major race or competition within 3-5 days
extracted_rules.action: Extend sleep by 1-2 hours per night for 3-5 nights before the event; bank sleep in advance of potential pre-race night disruption
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Pre-competition: +1 to +2h sleep/night for 3-5 nights before event; race-night sleep quality matters less than pre-race banking
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: sleep and ultra-endurance performance (original source); pre-competition sleep banking research

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete experiences cognitive fatigue during ultra-endurance competition (>6h event)
extracted_rules.action: Permit and schedule mid-race naps of 20-30 minutes at planned aid stations; napping improves subsequent decision-making and reduces error rate
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Nap duration: 20-30 min (avoid >30 min to prevent sleep inertia); improvement in cognitive performance: significant vs. no nap
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: sleep role in ultra-endurance performance (original source)

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete's schedule includes early morning practice sessions (before 6am)
extracted_rules.action: Flag as a risk factor for sleep disturbance; recommend earlier bedtime, not reduced wake time; monitor weekly sleep totals
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Early practice (pre-6am): flag circadian risk; bedtime advance: shift bedtime by 30-60 min to compensate; monitor total weekly sleep hours
extracted_rules.source: Review: circadian rhythm and sleep disturbances in young adult athletes (original source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Sleep quality consequences — performance and mental health
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete reports or shows signs of inadequate sleep
extracted_rules.action: Assess for reduced reaction times and mental health indicators (depressive symptoms, anxiety); modify training load and escalate if mental health signs present
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Inadequate sleep → reduced reaction time, anxiety, depressive symptoms; training load reduction: 15-20% until sleep normalizes
extracted_rules.source: Review: circadian rhythm and sleep disturbances in young adult athletes (original source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Yoga and meditation for melatonin and sleep quality
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete seeks a non-pharmacological approach to improve sleep onset and quality
extracted_rules.action: Prescribe 20-30 min of yoga or meditation in the evening; proven to elevate melatonin and improve sleep quality
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Yogic techniques (meditation + mantra): pooled SMD = 0.37 (95% CI: 0.09-0.66) on melatonin levels; practice ≥3×/week
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: yogic techniques and melatonin levels (original source)

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete or individual experiences general sleep disturbances (not from overtraining)
extracted_rules.action: Regular moderate aerobic exercise improves sleep quality; schedule exercise ≥4 hours before bedtime to avoid sleep disruption
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Aerobic exercise: widely supported for sleep quality improvement; timing: ≥4h before bedtime; morning or afternoon preferred
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: aerobic exercise and sleep quality (original source)

extracted_rules.condition: If athlete shows any of the following signs after a heavy training block: RPE inflation, mood disturbance, or reduced motivation
extracted_rules.action: Assess for CNS fatigue; reduce CNS-demanding sessions; increase recovery days until indicators resolve
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: CNS fatigue signs: RPE +2 or more vs. expected at given pace/load; reaction time increase >10%; mood disturbance; motivation loss; if ≥2 signs: mandatory 48-72h CNS recovery
extracted_rules.source: Overreaching and CNS fatigue research; Meeusen et al. 2013 overtraining consensus statement

extracted_rules.condition: After a heavy maximal strength session (≥85% 1RM, compound lifts, CNS-intensive)
extracted_rules.action: Do not schedule another CNS-demanding session within 48-72 hours; this includes maximal sprint efforts, heavy Olympic lifts, and high-intensity intervals
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Post-maximal strength CNS recovery: 48-72h before next CNS-demanding session; aerobic Z1-Z2 work permitted within 24h
extracted_rules.source: Neuromuscular fatigue research; Aagaard 2003; Häkkinen et al. CNS recovery studies

extracted_rules.rule_name: Caffeine timing — avoid CNS disruption to sleep
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete uses caffeine for performance enhancement
extracted_rules.action: Schedule last caffeine dose ≥6 hours before planned bedtime; athletes sensitive to caffeine should avoid post-2pm consumption
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Caffeine half-life: ~5-6h; last dose: ≥6h pre-sleep; for sensitive athletes: cut-off at 2pm; avoid late training sessions with pre-workout caffeine
extracted_rules.source: Caffeine pharmacokinetics research; sleep-caffeine interaction consensus

extracted_rules.rule_name: Pfitz 'rest is training' — prioritize recovery over forced sessions
extracted_rules.condition: When an athlete shows poor recovery signals (low HRV, high fatigue, poor sleep) before a scheduled hard session
extracted_rules.action: Replace the planned hard session with rest or easy movement; recovery is the adaptation stimulus, not the session itself; unscheduled rest > forced hard session
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: If ≥2 of: RMSSD <15ms, sleep <6h, subjective fatigue ≥4/5, RPE inflation >2: replace hard session with Z1 ≤45 min or full rest; adaptation occurs during recovery, not during the session
extracted_rules.source: Pfitzinger & Douglas, Advanced Marathoning (rest as training); Daniels' Running Formula §2 rest principle

extracted_rules.condition: In the 1-2 weeks following a marathon or endurance event >3 hours
extracted_rules.action: Prescribe elevated sleep target; expect athlete to need more sleep than their normal baseline due to systemic inflammation and muscle repair
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Post-marathon weeks 1-2: target 9-10h sleep/night (vs normal 8h target); elevated sleep need driven by systemic inflammation; do not penalize as laziness — it is appropriate physiological response
extracted_rules.source: Pfitzinger & Douglas, Advanced Marathoning; post-marathon recovery physiology research

extracted_rules.rule_name: Daniels 3-4 week hold — CNS consolidation principle
extracted_rules.condition: When deciding whether to increase training load after a period of consistent training
extracted_rules.action: Hold training volume at current level for 3-4 weeks before increasing; this is CNS and musculoskeletal consolidation, not just muscular adaptation
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Volume hold: 3-4 weeks at consistent level before increasing; rationale: neuromuscular, tendon, and bone adaptations lag muscular adaptations by 2-4 weeks; premature increase = elevated injury risk
extracted_rules.source: Daniels' Running Formula §2 Volume Progression; bone and tendon adaptation research

extracted_rules.rule_name: Sleep deprivation — RPE inflation mechanism (5–6h sleep)
extracted_rules.condition: If athlete slept 5–6 hours the night before a planned session (below minimum threshold; session is being undertaken with Rule 1 modification active)
extracted_rules.action: Within the 20% intensity reduction prescribed by Rule 1, expect RPE to be artificially inflated; reduce pace/power targets by 5-10% from planned to compensate; this is the RPE inflation mechanism — the session proceeds but with adjusted targets, not raw feel-based effort
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 5–6h sleep: RPE inflated +1 to +2 vs. normal; pace/power target reduction: 5–10% from planned (implements Rule 1's 20% intensity reduction); adjusted session > no session for adaptation signal; for sleep <5h: Rule 1 critical flag applies — no high-intensity sessions, full rest preferred
extracted_rules.source: Fullagar et al. 2015 sleep and athletic performance; Skein et al. 2011 sleep and sport performance

extracted_rules.rule_name: Screen and blue-light cutoff for sleep quality
extracted_rules.condition: When advising an athlete on sleep hygiene practices
extracted_rules.action: Prescribe screen/blue-light avoidance 60-90 minutes before planned bedtime; blue light suppresses melatonin onset
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Screen cutoff: 60-90 min before bedtime; blue light (wavelength 460-480 nm) suppresses melatonin by 50-60%; alternatives: blue-light blocking glasses (partial effect), dim red/warm lighting
extracted_rules.source: Chang et al. 2015 blue-light and melatonin; Harvard chronobiology lab sleep hygiene research

extracted_rules.condition: When prescribing naps to supplement nocturnal sleep or for performance recovery
extracted_rules.action: Use power naps (10-20 min) or full-cycle naps (90 min); avoid the 20-90 min sleep inertia zone; schedule before 3pm to minimize circadian disruption
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Power nap: 10-20 min (light sleep + REM; no inertia); Full-cycle nap: 90 min (complete sleep cycle; maximal restoration); AVOID: 21-89 min naps (deep NREM onset = strong sleep inertia); all naps: schedule before 3pm
extracted_rules.source: Mednick et al. 2002 nap optimization; sleep architecture research; Waterhouse et al. 2007
